coredata.dc:title: Photophysical properties of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots synthesized by graphite
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of The Chinese Ceramic Society.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of The Chinese Ceramic Society.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jia
Yu, Hailong
Wang, Guan
Yang, Shuang
Li, Meichen
Zhang, Xin
Jia, Yang
Gao, Yachen
coredata.dc:description: Here, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were successfully synthesized by the solvothermal method using graphite as the carbon source and N,N-dimethylformamide as the nitrogen source. We characterized the structure and chemical constitution of N-CQDs using X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. We investigated the pump- and temperature-dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties and the dynamic exciton recombination processes of N-CQDs, using both steady-state and time-resolved PL techniques. The spectral results show that the PL emission peak located at 518 nm at room temperature, mainly originates from the n-π∗ transition on the surface of N-CQDs. The pump fluence and PL integral intensity were analyzed to demonstrate the existence of single-photon excitation under the 405 nm laser excitation. As the temperature increases, the non-radiative transition gradually increases, which decreases the PL intensity, the full width at half maxima first narrows and then widens and the PL lifetime gradually decreases. Furthermore, we combined the N-CQDs with chip to prepare light-emitting diode (LED). The resulting chromaticity coordinate was obtained to be (0.29, 0.40). This study offers a comprehensive understanding of the luminescence mechanism in N-doped CQDs and introduces a novel approach for the quickly fabrication of full-color display LEDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Nitrogen-doped
Photoluminescence
Temperature-dependent
Non-radiative transition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images, (c) XRD patterns, and (d) FT-IR images of N-CQDs, the inset is partial enlargement of 3,560 cm−1.
XPS spectra. (a) Survey spectrum of N-CQDs, the inset is the element analysis, (b) spectra of C 1s, (c) spectra of N 1s, (d) spectra of O 1s.
(a) Absorption spectra at 300 K, the insets show optical pictures under daylight and 365 nm UV-light. (b) Tauc plot of N-CQDs.
(a) PL at various pump fluences. (b) Linear fitting between PL intensities and pump fluences. (c) Luminescence mechanism of the N-CQDs.
(a) 2D color map of TRPL spectra. (b) TRPL spectrum from 0 to 12 ns. (c) PL dynamics at 500 nm.

(a) Color map related to power fluence at 80 K. (b) Emission characteristics under the influence of different excitation energy at 80 K. (c) Variation pattern of emission intensity with system excitation pump fluence. (d) Tauc plot of N-CQDs, the inset shows UV–Vis absorption spectrum at 80 K.
(a) Normalized PL spectra at different temperature. (b) Normalized PL intensity and (c) FWHM at different temperature.
(a) Temperature-dependent PL decay curves. (b) Scheme diagram of temperature-dependent energy state and PL dynamics mechanism.
Application of N-CQDs. (a) Emission spectra of CQDs LED (insets are the photo graphics schematic of the LED). (b) Corresponding chromaticity coordinates.
Comparison of the designed LED with previous LEDs. (Red, Green, Blue, Yellow and Orange-CQDs are abbreviated as R, G, B, Y and O-CQDs).

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of The Chinese Ceramic Society.

head.ce:title: Photophysical properties of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots synthesized by graphite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic Engineering, Heilongjiang University, Harbin, 150080, Heilongjiang, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronic Engineering, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, Heilongjiang, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Communication and Electronic Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161000, Heilongjiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Communication and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Communication and Electronic Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161000, Heilongjiang, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were successfully synthesized by the solvothermal method using graphite as the carbon source and N,N-dimethylformamide as the nitrogen source. We characterized the structure and chemical constitution of N-CQDs using X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. We investigated the pump- and temperature-dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties and the dynamic exciton recombination processes of N-CQDs, using both steady-state and time-resolved PL techniques. The spectral results show that the PL emission peak located at 518 nm at room temperature, mainly originates from the n-π∗ transition on the surface of N-CQDs. The pump fluence and PL integral intensity were analyzed to demonstrate the existence of single-photon excitation under the 405 nm laser excitation. As the temperature increases, the non-radiative transition gradually increases, which decreases the PL intensity, the full width at half maxima first narrows and then widens and the PL lifetime gradually decreases. Furthermore, we combined the N-CQDs with chip to prepare light-emitting diode (LED). The resulting chromaticity coordinate was obtained to be (0.29, 0.40). This study offers a comprehensive understanding of the luminescence mechanism in N-doped CQDs and introduces a novel approach for the quickly fabrication of full-color display LEDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were first discovered in 2004 [1], marked the introduction of a new member to the family of carbon nanomaterials and subsequently becoming a hot topic in research. CQDs have been intensively studied in the fields of fluorescence sensing and light-emitting diodes (LED) [2,3] owing to their high optical stability and cost-effectiveness [4,5]. The synthesis methods for CQDs include both top-down and bottom-up [6,7]. However, CQDs prepared by the bottom-up method has become the choice of more and more researchers because of the high fluorescence efficiency and the absence of by-product generation [8,9]. Among them, the solvothermal method is a typical bottom-up method and has been widely used for its advantages of low energy consumption, less agglomeration, and controllable components [10].

In 2017, Qiu et al. synthesized CQDs using the solvothermal method in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The prepared CQDs showed blue fluorescence emission and high quantum yield of 47% [11]. In 2019, Zhao et al. prepared red CQDs in DMF using l-methionine and urea. The CQDs had a quantum yield of 61% and applied this to the detection of Fe3+ [12]. In 2021, Rao et al. synthesized red CQDs using ammonium citrate and DMF and obtained one-component white light by diluting the stock solution with DMF. This solvent engineering could facilitate the application of carbon-based materials in optoelectronic devices [13]. In 2022, Kim et al. performed hydrothermal synthesis using citric acid and urea in DMF. Polychromatic luminescent CQDs were purified using column chromatography and their full width at half maxima (FWHM) was reduced by 42 nm by diluting with a strong acid [14].

In reports above, the carbon sources used for preparing N-CQDs were generally chemical reagents or materials obtained directly from nature. This led to increased costs and the introduction of new impurities, which affected the large-scale preparation and the photophysical properties of the N-CQDs themselves. On the other side, it is necessary to conduct systematical research on the photoluminescence (PL) of N-CQDs for purpose of various applications. In our paper, N-CQDs were prepared by solvothermal method through grinding bulk graphite. To reveal the luminescence mechanism of N-CQDs, we performed morphological and optical characterization, respectively. The pump- and temperature-dependent PL were studied systematically. The effect of non-radiative transition processes on the fluorescence of N-CQDs was verified through temperature-dependent steady-state and time-resolved PL (TRPL). Finally, we also prepared LED with corresponding wavelengths and analyzed the luminescence characteristics of N-CQDs into the LED.

The graphite used in this study was sourced from Beike 2D materials Co., Ltd. DMF was acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine was sourced from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. All chemicals were not subjected to secondary treatment.

The N-CQDs were synthesized by solvothermal method [11], as shown in Fig. 1 . The graphite was grinded into powder using a porcelain mortar. 0.2 g graphite powder was added to 20 mL of DMF. The sample was black before reaction, and there was no fluoresce under laser excitation. Then, the mixture was transferred for solvothermal reaction and heated to 180 °C and held 12 h. It was cooled naturally to 25 °C, centrifugation (10,000 r/min, for 15 min) and then supernatant was taken to obtain N-CQDs solution. The prepared N-CQDs show transparent yellowish color in daylight and green emission under laser excitation.

The morphology and size of the N-CQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), while the lattice structure was characterized by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The structure and elementary composition of N-CQDs were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The absorption spectra were obtained using a UV–Visible spectrophotometer (TU-1901, Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd).

A 405 nm continuous-wave (CW) laser (MLL-Ⅲ, CNI) was used as the excitation source, and the PL spectra were collected using a fiber optic spectrometer (USB4000, Ocean Optics). For TRPL spectra testing, a 405 nm, 200 ps ps laser (MDL-PS-405, CNI) was used as the excitation source. The TRPL spectra were recorded using a spectrometer (SP-2500, Princeton Instruments) paired with an intensified charge coupled device (ICCD) detector (PI-MAX-1024i, Princeton Instruments). For temperature-dependent steady-state PL and TRPL tests, we use a vacuum liquid nitrogen cryostat (Cryo-77, Oriental Koji) for temperature control ranging from 80 K to 380 K. The PL dynamics were further measured using a time correlated single photo counter (TCSPC) system. The data collection for this system was performed using a designated monochromator (SPCM-01-20, Holita) and a single photo counting electronic module (FLA-130, Holita).

The N-CQDs were mixed with N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine and allowed to stand for 5 h to form a homogeneous gel-like liquid [15]. This gel-like liquid was applied dropwise to the 395 nm UV lamp chips to create the monochromatic LEDs, which were then placed in a drying oven for 3 h. The LED drive current was supplied through a CC&CV DC POWER SUPPLY (Hong Pu Optoelectronics Technology Co., Ltd.).

As presented in Fig. 2 a, the size and morphology of the N-CQDs were observed by TEM. Apparently, the N-CQDs are equally distributed and spherical, with no obvious aggregation. The average particle size is approximately 3 nm. From HRTEM in Fig. 2b, the space of lattice fringes can be found clearly to be 0.21 nm. These fringes primarily correspond to the (100) lattice planes of graphite, demonstrating that all N-CQDs have a similar structure to graphite [16]. Fig. 2c presents the XRD patterns of the N-CQDs with a broad diffraction peak at 20.58° and an insignificant diffraction peak at 26.5°, both of which correspond to the (002) crystal plane of graphite. The above lattice contraction is typical character of nanomaterials, and suggests that the N-CQDs have an amorphous structure with graphitic carbon sp2 hybridized carbon cores [17,18]. To identify the chemical groups of the N-CQDs, we performed FT-IR characterization displayed in Fig. 2d. The absorption band at 3,560 cm−1 corresponds to the OH/NH stretching vibrations in the hydroxyl and amino groups. The peaks at 2,927.46 cm−1 represents CH stretching vibration, while those at 1,660.34 cm−1 and 1,502.73 cm−1 origin from CO/CC and NH stretching vibrations, respectively [12]. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1,256.65 cm−1 and 1,087.99 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibration of aromatic CN [19]. Finally, the absorption at 864.01 cm−1 and 655.84 cm−1 arises from the CH out-of-plane bending vibrational absorption [20]. These vibrational modes are all consistent with the common features of N-CQDs, which demonstrate the existence of carboxylic acids, hydroxyl groups and amino groups upon solvothermal [21].

Furthermore, the chemical composition of N-CQDs was further analyzed using XPS, and XPS spectra of N-CQDs were given in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3a, at 284.85, 399.6 eV and 531.8 eV, there are three distinct peaks which correspond to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The atomic ratios are obtained to be 88.91%, 4.36% and 6.74%, respectively. In addition, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum was depicted in Fig. 3b, and it can be deconvoluted into three single peaks which are attributed to CC/CC (284.82 eV), CN (285.14 eV) and CO (288.13 eV) bonding. The N 1s spectrum, shown in Fig. 3c, is dominated by the pyrrole state N (399.63 eV). This indicates a potential shift in the edge state of N-CQDs, transitioning from a carboxyl group to a conjugated pyrrole or pyrrolidinone group, which is referred to as the N state [22]. As presented in Fig. 3d, the O 1s spectrum displays two peaks corresponding to CO (531.59 eV) and OH (532.26 eV) [19].

The absorption spectra of the N-CQDs are presented in Fig. 4 a, where the N-CQDs exhibit a sharp absorption peak at 300 nm, which is caused by the π-π∗ transition in the core aromatic domain CC bond [23]. This absorption suggests a transition from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [24]. In addition, a shoulder peak observed at 370 nm arises from the n-π∗ transitions associated with the CO bond on the particles surface. This observation is consistent with the FT-IR results. The inset of Fig. 4a presents an optical photograph of the sample appearing as a transparent yellowish liquid under daylight and emitting green in 365 nm UV-light. The forbidden bandwidth can be calculated via plot of (αhν)2 vs photon energy which is described by using the Tauc-plot formula [14]:
(1)
α h ν
1 / n
= A
h ν -
E g
where α is defined as the absorption coefficient, hν denotes the photo energy, A is the proportionality constant.

The plot of (αhν)2 vs. photon energy of the N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 4b. By extrapolating the straight-line segment of the plot, a crossover point is obtained. According to Equation (1), the horizontal coordinate of the crossover point is photon bandgap energy (E g) which is 3.50 eV [25].

As shown in Fig. 5 a, the PL spectrum was probed under 405 nm CW laser excitation with different pump fluence. It can be found there is a broadband emission peak is discernible at 518 nm. Moreover, the PL intensity exhibited gradual increased as the laser pump fluence ranged from 35 mW/cm2 to 5,729 mW/cm2. The logarithmic relationship between the PL intensity and pump fluence as presented in Fig. 5b. The calculated slope was 1.05, which confirms that the PL of N-CQDs is induced by single photon absorption under 405 nm CW laser excitation [26]. After the above spectral analysis, we propose the luminescence mechanism of N-CQDs and related relaxation process, as displayed in Fig. 5c. When the sample is excited by 405 nm (3.06 eV) CW laser (purple arrow), only the surface electrons can be excited to undergo a transition because the excitation energy is lower than the bandgap energy. When the electrons are excited from the ground state (N state) to the excited state (LUMO), they will rapidly relax to the edge state (black dotted arrow). When the excited electrons in the edge state spontaneously relax to the N state releases energy in the form of light (green arrow), causing the N-CQDs to produce a PL emission peak at 518 nm (2.39 eV) [22].

Fig. 6 a shows the N-CQDs nanosecond transient emission spectrum. Fig. 6b illustrates the PL spectral decay process from 0 to 12 ns. It is evident that the PL intensity gradually diminishes as the frame count rises. The PL decay curves for N-CQDs are given in Fig. 6c and its lifetimes were derived by fitting to the equation [27]:
(2)
I
t
=
I 0
e
-
t / τ
where I(t) is the emission intensity at time t, and I 0 is the emission intensity at t = 0, τ is the lifetime. By fitting the data in Fig. 6c, the PL lifetime is obtained to be 4.68 ns. The PL lifetime is exactly the relaxation time from the edge state to the N state in Fig. 5c (red dashed line). It also implies that it has better photostability and can resist unfavorable factors such as photodegradation, thus maintaining better optical performance. This is similar to previous reports [28]. The single exponential lifetime of N-CQDs suggests that their PL emission comes from a single luminescent species, which will be beneficial for the fabrication of pure-color LEDs.

To further understand the effect of temperature on the photophysical characterization of N-CQDs, we measured the luminescence spectra of N-CQDs at 80 K. Fig. 7 a presents the PL color map related to power density at 80 K. Fig. 7b presents the variation of PL intensity with pump power density. Compared with the case at 300 K, the PL peak of N-CQDs at 80 K shift from 518 nm to 508 nm, which means the energy gap becomes wide at relatively lower temperature [29]. Fig. 7d shows the bandgap of N-CQDs is 3.70 eV at 80 K, calculated from Equation (1). The inset shows the absorption spectra of N-CQDs, marked by a peak at 308 nm. In addition, the peak position does not move with the increased pump fluence which increases the PL intensity. Fig. 7c shows the relationship between PL intensity and pump fluence in logarithmic terms. It is clear that the relationship is linear and the slope of relationship line is about 1, indicating the PL of N-CQDs at low temperature of 80 K origins still from single photon absorbing.

Furthermore, we studied the PL properties of N-CQDs at other temperatures. Fig. 8 a shows the normalized PL spectra as the temperature range of 80 K–300 K. As evident from the data, there is a gradual decline in PL intensity with the rise in temperature.

Fig. 8b shows the normalized PL intensity at different temperature with interval of 10 K. The gradual decrease in PL intensity as rising temperature, which is believed to the result of a thermally activated non-radiative transition [30]. Specifically, with the temperature rises, non-radiative channels are activated due to thermal effects (thermal activation).

This thermal activation intensifies the non-radiative recombination of electrons and holes will become strong, which causes radiative recombination to reduce and PL to decrease. In fact, the process can be described quantitatively by using Arrhenius equation [31]:
(3)
I
T
=
I 0
/
1 + A
exp
-
E b
/
k B
T
where I 0 is the PL intensity at 0 K, E b is the exciton binding energy, k B is the Boltzmann constant, I(T) is defined as the PL intensity at temperature T, and A is the fitting parameter. The solid line in Fig. 8b is the theoretical fitting result via Equation (3). Correspondingly, E b was obtained to be 134.59 meV which is larger than the E b of many semiconductor QDs [32]. The larger E b means that the exciton is more stable, which is favorable for promoting radiative compounding.

To investigates the temperature's impact on the of PL linewidth, we extracted the FWHM of the PL spectrum from Fig. 8a. The fitted curve of FWHM versus temperature for N-CQDs is presented in Fig. 8c. We can observe that as the temperature raise from 80 K to 200 K, the FWHM of PL becomes slightly narrow. While when temperature increases from 200 K to 300 K, the FWHM broadens from 135 nm to 170 nm. The phenomenon can be analyzed by using Boson models [33]:
(4)
Γ
T
=
Γ 0
+
Γ op
/
exp
ħ
ω op
/
k B
T
− 1
where Г 0 is the non-uniform broadening at 0 K, which is temperature-independent. Г(T) is the linewidth at the temperature T. Г op is the exciton-longitudinal optical (LO) coupling strength. ħω op is the LO phonon energy. The solid line in Fig. 8c represents the fitting results of FWHM through Equation (4). Correspondingly, the ħω op of the N-CQDs was obtained to be 51.89 meV which is similar with that of semiconductor QDs [34].

To understand the PL dynamical processes in N-CQDs at different temperatures, by using time-dependent single-photon counting, we measured TRPL spectra shown in Fig. 9 a. The solid line in the figure is theoretical fit result obtained via Equation (2), and the PL lifetime of N-CQDs at different temperature is obtained. It was observed that the PL lifetime decreased from 10.77 ns to 4.25 ns as the temperature rises from 80 K to 300 K. The mechanism of temperature-dependent PL lifetime is analyzed in Fig. 9b. The N-CQDs are excited to the excited state (LUMO) when using 405 nm CW laser excitation. When at low temperatures, the electrons undergo radiative transition, radiating from the edge state to the surface N state with the fluorescence lifetime is 10.77 ns. As the increase of temperature, the non-radiative transitions between electrons increase due to the thermal activation effect and part of the energy is consumed, resulting in a decrease in the energy of the electrons undergoing radiative transitions and the fluorescence lifetime is 4.25 ns. This indicates that inelastic scattering of phonons on excitons at higher temperatures reduces the exciton lifetime [35].

In present, N-CQDs received wide attention for their applications including biological imaging, catalysis, LED and so on [36,37]. Herein a LED was prepared by combining the synthesized N-CQDs with the 395 nm UV lamp chips [38]. The luminescent properties of the LED were investigated. Fig. 10 a shows the emission spectra and optical photograph of the LED. It can be seen that the prepared LED has a wide emission spectrum. Typical white LEDs require a mixture of several colors of light-emitting materials. Fig. 10b shows the color coordinates of PL of the prepared LED. It can be seen the chromaticity coordinate (CIE) is (0.29, 0.40).

As shown in Table 1 , we compared our LED with previous white LEDs prepared using CQDs. The standard value of the CIE of white LEDs is (0.33, 0.33). We find that the all LEDs listed in Table 1 are close to this standard value. In previous studies, LEDs require several kinds CQDs mixed in a certain ratio to reach the value, which require several kinds of carbon sources. However, in our work, only one type of CQDs was used to make the LEDs, which is beneficial for the rapid preparation of LEDs. Meanwhile, we synthesized the N-CQDs using only graphite as carbon source, which is benefit for the mass and cost-saving production of LEDs.

In conclusion, we successfully synthesized N-CQDs in DMF using a simple solvothermal method. All excitation lights were 405 nm lasers. Firstly, the PL properties of N-CQDs were studied at room temperature. The experimental results show the PL of N-CQDs were induced by single-photon absorption. The PL decay curve is linear indicating single exponential lifetime of N-CQDs of 4.68 ns. This reveals that the PL emission of N-CQDs come from a sole luminescent species. In addition, the effect of temperature on the luminescence characteristics was investigated at temperature from 80 K to 300 K. The results show that the PL intensity of N-CQDs decreases gradually with increased temperature. The process was analyzed quantitatively by using Arrhenius equation, and the exciton binding energy E b is obtained to be 134.59 meV. The temperature-dependent PL linewidth is theoretically analyzed using the boson model, and the LO phonon energy is 51.89 meV. It was observed that the PL lifetime decreased from 10.77 ns to 4.25 ns as the temperature rises from 80 K to 300 K, which indicates that the inelastic scattering of the phonon on the exciton decreases the lifetime of the exciton at higher temperatures. Finally, we successfully engineered and assessed white LEDs utilizing N-CQDs.

Jia Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing-original draft, preparation. Yachen Gao: Writing-review & editing, Supervision. Hailong Yu: Writing - review & editing. Guan Wang: Validation. Shuang Yang: Supervision. Meichen Li: Software. Xin Zhang: Investigation. Yang Jia: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This paper is grateful for the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2020F041) and Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities [145209148].

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yachen Gao is a professor and doctoral supervisor, the director of the Department of Optoelectronics in the College of Electronic Engineering, Heilongjiang University. He is mainly engaged in the study of optical nonlinear and nanophotonic.

Jia Liu is currently pursuing a Ph.D. degree in Electronic Science and Technology at Heilongjiang University. Her main research interests are the optical properties of carbon quantum dots and their applications in light-emitting devices and sensing detection.
